text:
ByMichelle Bruton,

Contributor.

“A breath of fresh air.”

“A reset.”

“A weight off our shoulders.”

These were just some of the ways Olympic skateboarders described their first competition since the Paris Games at the inaugural Rockstar Energy Open, which was held in Portland August 23–25.

True to its name, the event aimed to give up-and-coming skateboarders and pros who don’t always make limited invite-only fields in other competitions an opportunity to compete.

For some, it was their first pro-level contest—and they capitalized on the chance, like Lazer Crawford did when he beat out five Paris 2024 Olympians to win the men’s street final.

Multiple amateurs, like 16-year-old Secret Lynn, who earned a spot in the competition through the event’s Video Qualifying Series, made it to the final, earning invaluable exposure and experience.

“I think a lot of kids think the best way to do it is to go straight into the Olympic qualifiers, and that’s not a bad thing, but it definitely helps having some less strenuous contests, less pressure, and kind of ease into it,” said Tate Carew, who competed on the U.S. men’s park team at Paris 2024.

For Carew and the dozens of other invited skateboarders coming off the two-year process that is qualifying for and competing in the Olympics, the Rockstar Energy Open represented a return to their roots—a core skateboarding contest without too much at stake.

“A lot of us just feel like a weight has been taken off our shoulders,” said two-time Olympic gold medalist Keegan Palmer, who competes for Australia.

“It’s a lot more relaxed. If I didn’t make it to finals [here], I’d still go to sleep fine. But if I didn’t make it to the Olympics, I’m gonna be crying at home,” Palmer added, laughing.

“This is like what I was kind of introduced to as contest skating, was contests like this, so it’s nostalgic for me, like going back to the old days,” Paris 2024 silver medalist Tom Schaar said.

Schaar, who landed the first 1080 in skateboarding in 2012 at the age of 12, established his transition skating prowess at Vans Pool Party and other core contests.

“Everyone that used to do the old contests is here skating too, so it’s more relaxed, no pressure, just having fun,” Schaar added.

The men’s park final at the Paris Olympics was the highest level of transition skating the sport has seen. Medalists Palmer, Schaar and Brazil’s Augusto Akio put down technical and highly difficult runs. Schaar’s silver-medal run included a heelflip Indy, an alley oop 540 and a kickflip Indy to fakie—so good it elicited fist pumps from Tony Hawk, who was sitting in the crowd and whose Birdhouse Skateboards team Schaar rides for.

Compare the enormous Paris park course with its 18 features to the Rockstar Energy Open, where the bowl was smaller and more straightforward—just a back-to-basics backyard setup. The men didn’t wear pads, because this wasn’t that kind of contest. As a result, the tricks weren’t as big (though Rockstar Energy Open winner Yuro Nagahara still managed a kickflip Indy transfer off the extension wall and a huge backside 540).

As Carew put it, “Here it’s much more about style.”

For Minna Stess, who represented the U.S. at the Paris Games, it was “such a breath of fresh air” to skate in a contest and not have to worry about earning enough Olympic qualifying points to be one of the top three Americans in the world rankings.

“I’ve never had this much fun at a contest,” Stess said.

Stess finished just off the podium in fourth at the Rockstar Energy Open, but she still accomplished what she set out to do.

“I’m just happy to put down a run because I didn’t get to put one down in Paris and I was kind of bummed,” she said. “My only goal for any contest is just to land a run and that’s what I did, so I’m like okay, third, fourth—I don’t care. As long as I put down what I wanted to.”

“It’s different from the Olympics because the Olympics is more like you have to really focus on yourself,” two-time U.S. Olympian Bryce Wettstein said about the environment at the Rockstar Energy Open.

“It’s just so much fun to be able to make everyone happy around you,” she added. “You drop in and you’re like, ‘Oh my gosh, this is so much bigger than myself.’ So even though you want to get up there [on the podium], you just want to make it fun and feel good. At the end of the day, it’s a dream to be able to compete on your skateboard.”

Chris Joslin, who was one of three Americans who competed in men’s street at the Paris Olympics, is a threat to podium in any contest he enters. But his career has been built filming video parts in the streets.

The Olympics were “an experience, to say the least,” Joslin said, but ultimately not what he’s looking for out of skateboarding.

“The Olympics felt like if I ever went to college, that’s what I envisioned it would have felt like,” Joslin said. “Just a bunch of kids trying to get a gold medal and they were there for one thing and one thing only, and it just wasn’t really my vibe.”

The Olympic qualification process is a grind, and Joslin was struggling with being away from his two young kids and with the question,Why am I here?

“I almost flew home from one of the qualifiers [in Shanghai] that I actually ended up coming in third place at, and when I was talking about flying home one of the skaters was like, ‘Oh man, you don’t realize how much you inspire people here, myself included,’” Joslin said.

He was floored. “I was like, ‘I’ve barely ever talked to this dude.’”

Joslin felt like his late grandma, who raised him and his two siblings, would have wanted him to pursue the experience. “It’s like they say, your angels will give you messages through people sometimes,” Joslin said.

“I’ll definitely keep skating these contests, because it’s kind of like, what else are you gonna be doing anyways? It’s part of the career,” Joslin said. “But my heart definitely remains in the streets for sure.”

Contests like the Rockstar Energy Open can bridge the gap between a standardized and sanitized event, like the Olympics, and the freedom and creativity of filming.

Prioritizing style, catching up with old friends, basking in the good vibes—that was the order of the day in Portland. But that doesn’t mean the competitive fire was absent.

Palmer, who has traveled to Ibiza, Australia and finally to Portland since the Paris Games wrapped, arrived to the Rockstar Energy Open fresh off a 17-hour flight and barely skated in practice.

“I was just really enjoying my life and relaxing,” Palmer said of his mindset after arriving in Portland. He and his fellow park skaters picked up the high-stakes hacky sack games they’d been having in Paris and went out in Portland until the wee hours.

But when the clock starts in the contest, the focus narrows. Palmer’s run, which included a kickflip Indy over the hip and an Indy 540, was good enough to land him on the podium, in third.

“You always want to win every contest,” Palmer said. “[Finishing] one, two or three, I’m happy, so I’ll take it for sure.”
title: Olympic Skateboarders Return To Their Roots At Rockstar Energy Open
author: Michelle Bruton
description: The skateboarders who arrived in Portland for the inaugural Rockstar Energy Open fresh off the Paris Olympics found a low-pressure, "refreshing" contest environment.
keywords: keegan palmer,Yuro Nagahara,rockstar energy open,rockstar open,lazer crawford,2028 summer olympics,olympic skateboarding,tom schaar,skateboarding portland,chris joslin
